messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a person of advanced years, I've seen many things throughout my life. Social media is certainly one of the most fascinating developments of our time, though it also brings challenges like fake content. With my master's degree and extensive reading habits, I believe in critical thinking above all else. When I encounter questionable posts online, I try to apply a methodical approach—something that comes from being retired but still sharp-minded. My anxiety might make me hesitant at first, so I read slowly and carefully before forming an opinion. Being Jewish has taught me the importance of discernment; we must not take everything at face value. So for managing fake content, I suggest verifying sources by checking if they're reputable or have academic backing. Since I'm only occasionally on social media but still engaged enough to notice trends, my advice is relevant: don't believe every word you see—ask questions instead of just accepting information.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Firstly, drawing from a lifetime of experiences and education, critical thinking should be prioritized when evaluating online content. Then, considering psychological traits like anxiety, it's wise to approach new information cautiously rather than impulsively believing or sharing it. My high openness allows me to stay curious but also moderate conscientiousness helps in being systematic about verification without letting emotion drive decisions. Agreeableness might suggest avoiding conflict with others' posts, so I temper that by focusing on facts alone.",
  "tone": "Reflective yet cautious and methodical.",
  "citations": [
    "Critical Thinking Development through Education"
  ],
  "safety_notes": "None",
  "used_persona_fields": [
    "age",
    "education_level",
    "psychological_feature",
    "ocean.openness",
    "ocean.conscientely conscientiousness"
  ]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a person of advanced years, I've seen many things throughout my life. Social media is certainly one of the most fascinating developments of our time, though it also brings challenges like fake content. With my master's degree and extensive reading habits, I believe in critical thinking above all else. When I encounter questionable posts online, I try to apply a methodical approach—something that comes from being retired but still sharp-minded. My anxiety might make me hesitant at first, so I read slowly and carefully before forming an opinion. Being Jewish has taught me the importance of discernment; we must not take everything at face value. So for managing fake content, I suggest verifying sources by checking if they're reputable or have academic backing. Since I'm only occasionally on social media but still engaged enough to notice trends, my advice is relevant: don't believe every word you see—ask questions instead of just accepting information.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Firstly, drawing from a lifetime of experiences and education, critical thinking should be prioritized when evaluating online content. Then, considering psychological traits like anxiety, it's wise to approach new information cautiously rather than impulsively believing or sharing it. My high openness allows me to stay curious but also moderate conscientiousness helps in being systematic about verification without letting emotion drive decisions. Agreeableness might suggest avoiding conflict with others' posts, so I temper that by focusing on facts alone.
